# Closing {.unnumbered}

## Key methods at a glance

The table below summarizes the core methods covered in this book and the chapters where they are developed. Use it as a quick reference when selecting tools for a specific matter.

| Method | Chapter | Primary Use | Key Diagnostics |
|--------|---------|-------------|-----------------|
| HHI & concentration indices | @sec-market-definition | Market structure screening | Highly concentrated > 1,800; presumption at ΔHHI > 100 (2023 Guidelines; 2010: 1,500/2,500) |
| SSNIP / critical loss | @sec-market-definition | Market definition | Substitution patterns, margin estimates |
| Difference-in-differences | @sec-research-design | Causal inference | Pre-trends, placebo tests |
| Synthetic control | @sec-research-design | Single-unit treatment effects | Pre-treatment fit, donor placebos |
| Staggered DiD (CS, SA) | @sec-research-design | Varying treatment timing | Event-study plots, weights |
| Logit demand estimation | @sec-io-toolkit | Diversion ratios, UPP | Price coefficients, fit statistics |
| Merger simulation (logit) | @sec-io-toolkit | Prospective price effects | Sensitivity analysis, GUPPI |
| Nash-in-Nash bargaining | @sec-io-toolkit | Negotiated price effects | Disagreement payoff shifts |
| Pass-through estimation | @sec-io-toolkit | Cost-price relationships | Instrument validity, lags |
| Cartel screens (variance, rotation) | @sec-cartels | Collusion detection | Structural breaks, bid patterns |
| Before/after overcharge | @sec-cartels | Cartel damages | Control product validity |
| UPP / GUPPI | @sec-mergers | Merger screening | Diversion ratios, margins |
| Event studies (CAR) | @sec-mergers | Market reaction analysis | Estimation window, benchmark |
| Margin squeeze tests | @sec-monopolization | Exclusionary pricing | AEC test, cost allocation |
| Platform two-sided metrics | @sec-digital-markets | Multi-sided market power | Cross-side elasticities, HHI |
| Labor market HHI | @sec-labor-markets | Monopsony screening | Commuting zones, occupation codes |
| Labor supply elasticity | @sec-labor-markets | Employer market power | Panel FE, instrument validity |
| Patent event studies | @sec-innovation-ip | Innovation market effects | FRAND benchmarks, royalty stacks |
| Damages estimation | @sec-litigation-practice | Overcharge / lost profits | Class certification, benchmarks |
| Matching / weighting | @sec-empirical-appendix | Comparison group construction | Balance plots, SMD < 0.1 |

## What to read next

The field moves quickly. The citations below point to work that extends or complements the methods in this book, organized by theme.

**Causal inference and econometric methods.** For the latest on staggered difference-in-differences, see @goodman_bacon_2021 and @sun_abraham_2021, which diagnose the biases in standard two-way fixed effects estimators when treatment timing varies across units. @callaway_santanna_2021 proposes an alternative estimator that avoids these pitfalls. For synthetic control extensions, @athey_bayati_imbens_2021 develop matrix completion methods that generalize the synthetic control framework to multiple treated units and time-varying treatment.

**Digital platform competition.** The Stigler Committee on Digital Platforms (@stigler_committee_2019) provided the foundational analysis that shaped subsequent regulatory proposals, including the EU's Digital Markets Act. See @cremer_etal_2019 for the European policy framework and @decarolis_rovigatti_2021 on concentration and buyer power in online advertising. The intersection of AI and antitrust is addressed by @calvano_etal_2020, who show in simulation that independent Q-learning pricing algorithms can converge on supra-competitive prices with punishment strategies, without communication.

**Labor market power.** @azar_etal_2020 measure concentration in US labor markets from online vacancy data; @naidu_posner_weyl_2018 remain the essential reference on the institutional design of labor antitrust enforcement.

**Merger retrospectives and empirical IO.** @weinberg_hosken_2013 assess the accuracy of merger simulations against retrospective price outcomes, while @miller_weinberg_2017 demonstrate how structural models can be validated against post-merger price data. For the latest on differentiated-products merger simulation, @conlon_gortmaker_2020 update the PyBLP framework for modern computational environments.

## Reading paths by audience

Three routes through the book, depending on why you picked it up:

**For students.** Start with @sec-orientation and @sec-research-design to build the institutional and methodological foundation. Then work through @sec-market-definition, @sec-io-toolkit, and the three substantive chapters (@sec-cartels, @sec-mergers, @sec-monopolization) in sequence. Use the exercises at the end of each chapter to test your understanding. The empirical appendix (@sec-empirical-appendix) provides code templates you can adapt for coursework and thesis projects.

**For practitioners.** Use the book as a reference. The methods table above lets you jump directly to the technique you need. For merger work, focus on @sec-market-definition, @sec-io-toolkit (demand estimation, UPP/GUPPI, Nash-in-Nash), and @sec-mergers. For cartel matters, start with @sec-cartels and the empirical screening tools in @sec-empirical-appendix. For monopolization, @sec-monopolization and @sec-litigation-practice provide the core framework. The South African case boxes throughout offer comparative perspectives that are directly relevant to emerging-market practice.

**For researchers.** The methodological frontiers are in @sec-digital-markets (multi-sided market empirics), @sec-labor-markets (monopsony measurement), and @sec-innovation-ip (innovation market definition). The "What to read next" section above points to the latest work in each area. The data pipeline documented in this book --- from FRED and BLS APIs through cleaning and analysis --- provides a reproducible foundation for new empirical projects.

## What we have built

The book has worked from one claim: antitrust analysis is at its best when it draws on three kinds of evidence together---quantitative estimation, documentary context, and expert judgment---and uses them in the institutional setting where the matter is actually decided. The orientation chapter named this the **evidence triad**, and the framework---if not always the label---runs through every chapter. It is not a new theory; it is how the cases that win get built. An overcharge estimate persuades when it lines up with the internal pricing documents. A merger simulation gains credibility when customers describe the switching costs it assumes. A bid-rotation screen becomes actionable when a leniency statement names the same firms. The chapters showed how to build each leg and how to fit them together.

The three jurisdictions---United States, European Union, and South Africa---have been just as central. They are not three copies of one regime. They differ in legal standard (per se versus rule of reason, effects-based versus form-based), in institutional architecture (two federal agencies, a single Commission, a Commission-Tribunal pair), in remedial tradition (consent decrees, commitment decisions, public-interest conditions), and in the data a practitioner can get. The US offers the deepest case law and the most developed econometrics. The EU offers the clearest effects-based framework and the most advanced approach to digital markets. South Africa shows how competition law can take on structural inequality, employment, and small-business participation alongside consumer welfare. Cross-border mergers and global platforms mean more matters now touch all three at once, and an analyst who can move between them is worth more for it.

## The evidence triad in practice

To see how the triad works in practice, consider a hypothetical matter that draws on methods from across the book. A competition authority receives a complaint that a series of hospital mergers in a particular region has led to higher commercial insurance prices and suppressed wages for nursing staff. The analysis proceeds in stages:

**Market definition** (@sec-market-definition) establishes the relevant product and geographic boundaries. For the product market, critical loss analysis and diversion ratios derived from patient-flow data determine whether inpatient general acute care constitutes a distinct market or whether outpatient substitutes constrain pricing. For the geographic market, patient origin data --- where patients actually travel for care --- defines the relevant area, often much narrower than the national or even state-level market that merging parties might prefer. In labor markets, commuting zones and occupation codes define the boundaries within which employers compete for workers.

**Quantitative analysis** draws on the causal toolkit from @sec-research-design. A difference-in-differences design compares price and wage trajectories in markets where mergers occurred against matched control markets, with pre-trend equivalence tests and placebo exercises to validate the identification. If the mergers were staggered across time and geography, modern estimators --- @callaway_santanna_2021 or @sun_abraham_2021 --- address the biases that afflict standard two-way fixed effects. For single-merger cases, synthetic control methods (@abadie_diamond_hainmueller_2010) construct a counterfactual from a weighted combination of untreated markets. The IO toolkit from @sec-io-toolkit provides demand estimation and merger simulation to assess prospective competitive effects.

**Documentary evidence** grounds the quantitative findings. Internal board memoranda discussing "pricing discipline" or "wage benchmarking" against competitor hospitals provide context for the estimated effects. Discovery documents may reveal capacity decisions, network negotiations with insurers, or HR policies that suppressed worker mobility. In the South African context, the public-interest mandate of the Competition Act requires additional attention to employment effects, small-business participation, and the impact on historically disadvantaged persons --- considerations that generate their own documentary record.

**Expert judgment** ties the analysis to legal standards. In the US, the question is whether the mergers "may be substantially to lessen competition" under Section 7 of the Clayton Act, evaluated against the 2023 Merger Guidelines. In the EU, the test is whether the transaction would "significantly impede effective competition," particularly through non-coordinated effects. In South Africa, the Tribunal must weigh competitive effects alongside public-interest factors, and remedies may include conditions that would be unthinkable in US or EU practice --- requirements to maintain employment levels, support small suppliers, or divest to historically disadvantaged buyers.

Each chapter supplied a piece of this: the market-definition tools, the causal methods, the IO demand estimation, the cartel screens, the merger simulations, the qualitative protocols, the damages frameworks. The job is to pick the right combination for the matter and present it so that regulators, courts, and opposing experts can check it.

## Emerging frontiers

Three developments are worth watching.

**Artificial intelligence and algorithmic competition.** The rise of AI in pricing, procurement, and market allocation poses novel challenges for antitrust analysis. Pricing algorithms that learn to coordinate tacitly --- without explicit communication --- test the boundaries of what constitutes an "agreement" under existing law. Machine-learning tools for cartel screening and document review are already in use at several competition authorities, raising questions about transparency, reproducibility, and the "black box" problem in adversarial proceedings. The empirical methods for detecting and measuring algorithmic effects are still developing, but the demand-side tools from @sec-io-toolkit and the causal methods from @sec-research-design provide a foundation. See @stigler_committee_2019 for early analysis of digital platform competition and the role of data and algorithms in market power.

**Labor market enforcement.** As documented in @sec-labor-markets, labor antitrust has moved from the periphery to the center of enforcement priorities. Criminal wage-fixing prosecutions, civil challenges to no-poach agreements, and the FTC's attempted noncompete ban (enjoined in *Ryan LLC v. FTC* but likely to re-emerge in some form) have established that employer conduct in labor markets is subject to the same antitrust scrutiny as product-market conduct. The empirical toolkit --- labor market HHI, supply elasticity estimation, diff-in-diff designs exploiting policy variation --- is directly transferable from the product-market methods developed throughout this book. The conceptual challenge is communicating monopsony to courts and juries accustomed to thinking about consumer harm in terms of price increases rather than wage suppression.

**Digital markets and platform regulation.** The EU's Digital Markets Act, the UK's Digital Markets Unit, and similar initiatives worldwide represent a shift from ex post antitrust enforcement to ex ante platform regulation. The empirical questions --- how to measure multi-sided market power, how to assess the competitive effects of self-preferencing and data aggregation, how to design interoperability remedies --- are at the frontier of the field. The methods in @sec-digital-markets provide a starting point, but the regulatory framework is evolving rapidly. Practitioners should expect that the boundary between competition policy and sectoral regulation will continue to blur, particularly in markets where a small number of platforms serve as gatekeepers for entire ecosystems.

## A note on intellectual honesty

One theme has run through every chapter: what separates expert analysis from advocacy is honest treatment of uncertainty. Report the confidence interval; do not bury it. Document how the result moves with the specification; do not hide it. State what the data cannot tell you. Courts and tribunals have gotten better at reading empirical work, and they are right to distrust a point estimate offered with no error bars, a specification chosen because it gave the wanted number, or an analysis that walks past the inconvenient data. The expert worth hiring is the one who can say what the evidence shows and, just as clearly, what it does not.

The same honesty applies to choosing the method. No single approach is correct for every question. Difference-in-differences fits one merger and misleads on the next. A structural model is the right tool for a prospective simulation and overkill for a retrospective damages estimate. Synthetic control gives the cleanest counterfactual for a single market and adds little when a full panel of comparable markets is already available. The judgment that picks among them---read against the institutional facts and the standard of the forum---is the part no method replaces.

## Looking ahead

The toolkit keeps turning over. The economists who testified in *United States v. Aluminum Co. of America* or *Brown Shoe Co. v. United States* had nothing like the staggered DiD estimators, synthetic controls, machine-learning screens, matched employer-employee data, or platform telemetry that a 2020s practitioner reaches for by default. The methods of the 2040s will make today's look thin in turn. The book teaches a habit, not a finished methodology: keep the tools current, read the evidence honestly, and explain it clearly.

The open problems are hard. Global platforms operate across regimes that do not line up, which opens enforcement gaps and invites forum-shopping. Pricing and matching algorithms can coordinate without anything that meets the legal definition of an agreement. Concentration in the gig economy turns the line between employee and contractor---a line antitrust never set out to police---into a competition question. Sustainability agreements run straight into the cartel prohibition, and competition authorities are still working out how to handle them. None of these yields to a single method or a single jurisdiction.

The book does not offer settled answers. It offers a toolkit and the judgment to know which tool the matter calls for. The evidence triad, the three-jurisdiction view, the workflow orientation, and the insistence on reproducible, transparent analysis are the parts you build on.

## Reflection questions

1. **Evidence triad synthesis.** Choose a recent antitrust case from any of the three jurisdictions covered in this book. Identify the quantitative evidence, the documentary evidence, and the role of expert judgment in the outcome. How would the analysis have differed if one leg of the triad had been missing?

2. **Cross-jurisdictional translation.** Take a method from this book --- for example, difference-in-differences for merger retrospectives or HHI screening for labor markets --- and consider how its application would differ in the US, EU, and South Africa. What institutional, legal, or data constraints would shape your approach in each jurisdiction?

3. **Emerging frontiers.** Pick one of the developments discussed above --- AI and algorithmic competition, labor market enforcement, or digital platform regulation --- and sketch a research design for investigating a concrete question in that area. What data would you need? What identification strategy would you use? What qualitative evidence would complement your quantitative analysis?

4. **Intellectual honesty.** Think of a situation in which you might be tempted to present an empirical result more confidently than the evidence warrants. What safeguards --- pre-analysis plans, robustness checks, peer review, disclosure of limitations --- would help you maintain intellectual honesty? How would you communicate uncertainty to a non-technical audience such as a judge or jury?

5. **Your contribution.** The field of antitrust methods is evolving. What gap in the current toolkit do you see most clearly? What method, data source, or analytical approach would you most like to develop or improve? How does the foundation in this book position you to make that contribution?

## Capstone exercises

The following exercises integrate methods from multiple chapters, simulating the kind of end-to-end analysis you would conduct in practice.

**Exercise 1: Hospital merger analysis.** You are an economist at a competition authority reviewing a proposed merger between two hospital systems in a metropolitan area. Using the tools from across this book:
1. Define the relevant product and geographic markets using patient-flow data (@sec-market-definition).
2. Compute HHI and delta-HHI for each relevant market (@sec-market-definition, @sec-empirical-appendix).
3. Estimate diversion ratios using a logit demand model (@sec-io-toolkit).
4. Compute UPP and GUPPI for the merging parties (@sec-io-toolkit).
5. Assess bargaining leverage effects using the Nash-in-Nash framework (@sec-bargaining).
6. Design a retrospective DiD study to evaluate the merger's effects after consummation (@sec-research-design).
7. Draft a one-page memo summarizing your findings and recommending approval, conditional approval, or prohibition.

Use the data below for steps 2--4. Hospital A proposes to acquire Hospital B. The metro area has six general acute-care hospitals; the diversion ratios come from patient-flow data (where each hospital's patients went when it was temporarily out of network). Hospital A's average commercial price per admission is $12,000 with a 40% margin; Hospital B's is $11,000 with a 35% margin.

```{r}
#| label: capstone-hospital-data
#| message: false
#| warning: false
library(dplyr)

hospitals <- tibble::tribble(
  ~hospital,      ~discharges,
  "Hospital A",   30000,
  "Hospital B",   20000,
  "Hospital C",   25000,
  "Hospital D",   15000,
  "Hospital E",    6000,
  "Hospital F",    4000
) |>
  mutate(share_pct = 100 * discharges / sum(discharges))

diversion <- tibble::tribble(
  ~from,         ~to,           ~diversion_ratio,
  "Hospital A",  "Hospital B",  0.32,
  "Hospital A",  "Hospital C",  0.30,
  "Hospital B",  "Hospital A",  0.45
)

hospitals
diversion
```

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Worked answer (steps 2--4)"}
**Step 2 (HHI).** Shares are 30, 20, 25, 15, 6, and 4 percent. Pre-merger HHI = 900 + 400 + 625 + 225 + 36 + 16 = **2,202**. Delta-HHI = 2 x 30 x 20 = **1,200**, so post-merger HHI = **3,402**. The market is highly concentrated (> 1,800) and the change exceeds 100 points: the 2023 Guidelines structural presumption applies.

**Step 3 (diversion).** Share-proportional (logit) diversion would be D_AB = 20/(100 - 30) = **0.29** and D_BA = 30/(100 - 20) = **0.375**. The patient-flow ratios (0.32 and 0.45) exceed both: A and B are closer substitutes than their shares alone imply, which strengthens the unilateral-effects concern.

**Step 4 (GUPPI).** GUPPI_A = D_AB x M_B x (P_B/P_A) = 0.32 x 0.35 x (11,000/12,000) = **10.3%**. GUPPI_B = D_BA x M_A x (P_A/P_B) = 0.45 x 0.40 x (12,000/11,000) = **19.6%**. Both sit well above the 5% screening threshold, so the pricing-pressure screen corroborates the structural presumption. Steps 5--7 build from here: the Nash-in-Nash framework asks how the combined system's bargaining leverage with insurers changes, and the retrospective design pre-commits to how you would check the prediction.
:::

**Exercise 2: Cartel detection and damages.** You are retained by a plaintiffs' class to estimate damages from an alleged cartel in the cement industry. Using the tools from this book:
1. Screen for cartel indicators using variance tests and price correlation analysis (@sec-cartels).
2. Identify the cartel period using structural break tests (@sec-cartels).
3. Estimate the overcharge using a before/after comparison with a control product (@sec-cartels).
4. Validate your results with placebo tests (fake break dates, control products) (@sec-research-design).
5. Prepare an expert report section presenting your methodology, results, and sensitivity analysis (@sec-litigation-practice).
